behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive errors.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is parser validation/error construction in a local function/object graph. No OS/network/subprocess dependency. A focused unit test can catch real failures (accepting invalid ranges, wrong tokenization, poor error text). Mocks are unnecessary here and do not materially hide the main failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Call the parser directly with invalid inputs (`month=13`, `day=32`, malformed fields like `* * *`), assert parse fails, and assert error includes field-specific reason and source position/context.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps, including last-day-of-month and nearest-weekday semantics.
behaviors.justification: Real risk is contract mismatch between parsed schedule objects and the next-run calculator (e.g., parser encodes `L`/`W` semantics differently than calculator expects). A pure unit on calculator internals can miss parser-calculator wiring defects. No external infra or LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Parse cron expressions using the real parser, feed resulting schedule objects into the real next-run calculator, compute next N run times from fixed UTC start times, and compare against golden timestamp sets for normal cases plus `L` and `W` edge cases.

behaviors.description: Overlap detector flags schedules that violate minimum gap and reports conflicting windows.
behaviors.justification: Failure often appears in interaction among schedule expansion, time-window generation, and overlap logic. Mocking one side can hide boundary bugs (inclusive/exclusive edges, window rounding). No required OS/network/subprocess/LLM.
behaviors.test_description: Build two real schedules via parser+calculator, generate fire windows over a horizon, run overlap detector with a 5-minute threshold, and assert both detection boolean and exact reported conflict intervals for near-boundary cases (4:59, 5:00, 5:01).

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling handles DST spring-forward gaps and fall-back ambiguities for a 2:30 AM job.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real timezone/DST rules as implemented by runtime + tzdata (an environment boundary). In-process mocks of timezone conversion can easily pass while production tzdata/version behavior fails. No LLM dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run scheduler end-to-end against real timezone database for at least one DST-observing zone (e.g., `America/New_York`): verify 2:30 AM behavior across spring-forward date (nonexistent local time) and fall-back date (ambiguous local time), asserting concrete emitted UTC timestamps and policy handling of skipped/duplicated local times.

behaviors.description: When next run is in the past, engine applies missed-run policy (skip vs backfill).
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is policy engine + schedule computation + persisted job state/clock inputs. A unit test of policy alone can miss wiring bugs (incorrectly comparing last-run, now, and computed next-run). Usually no external system requirement beyond local persistence abstractions.
behaviors.test_description: Using real scheduling and policy modules with local state storage (in-memory or test DB), seed a job whose next run is in the past, execute reconciliation once for `skip` and once for `backfill`, and assert emitted execution plan (zero catch-up vs expected backlog run set).
